summary: Stancil tests whether polite prompts improve ChatGPT performance and finds the opposite: neutral phrasing yields the best accuracy, while nice or mean tones slightly degrade results and increase verbosity. Despite the data, he frames the choice to be polite as a Pascal’s Wager: a tiny performance cost might be worth it if kindness matters in a future where AI could punish us.
money_quotes: The verdict: Nice guys finish last.
When asked to do something in a neutral tone, ChatGPT correctly completed the task 73.4 percent of the time.
Being nice has other negative effects as well.
Anybody who uses ChatGPT has to place a similar wager—do we choose to treat it well, or not?
Yes, there are thirty basis points of accuracy to be had by being unkind to ChatGPT, but there’s eternal bliss to had by being nice to it.
key_insight: Neutral prompts slightly outperform polite or harsh ones, but he jokes that politeness may still be rational if you treat it as a low-cost hedge against a sci‑fi future.
